narrative: Healthcare providers lose cash because revenue cycle work is a constant administrative grind across prior authorizations, denials, appeals, portals, faxed documents, payer rules, staff queues, and follow-up tasks. The Agent Factory story is that healthcare administration is full of narrow workflows where the model does not need to practice medicine. It needs to orchestrate paperwork, retrieve missing context, build packets, follow payer rules, submit through the right channels, monitor queue status, and route ambiguous cases with a full audit trail.
buyerPromise: We compress denial and prior-auth admin work, keep every case moving, and surface the exact evidence and next action for staff instead of leaving them to chase portals and faxes.

subCriteria.name: Packet assembly
subCriteria.rationale: Collecting and assembling evidence packets is high-volume, rule-based

subCriteria.name: Payer-rule matching
subCriteria.rationale: Payer-specific rules add complexity and require frequent updates

subCriteria.name: EHR integration
subCriteria.rationale: EHR and practice-management systems have varying API maturity

subCriteria.name: Payer portals
subCriteria.rationale: Fragmented payer portals with inconsistent interfaces

subCriteria.name: Fax/document systems
subCriteria.rationale: Fax and PDF-heavy flows are accessible but messy

subCriteria.name: Clinical documentation
subCriteria.rationale: Rich clinical records available for evidence assembly

subCriteria.name: Payer guidelines
subCriteria.rationale: Published payer criteria provide clear grounding

subCriteria.name: Denial patterns
subCriteria.rationale: Denial codes and remittance data reveal systematic patterns

subCriteria.name: HIPAA compliance
subCriteria.rationale: Strong regulatory framework with clear requirements

subCriteria.name: Clinical boundary
subCriteria.rationale: Must never cross into clinical decision-making

subCriteria.name: Audit requirements
subCriteria.rationale: Compliance and appeals require full audit trail

examples.name: Regional Hospital Prior Authorization Transformation
examples.source: Infinx case study on a regional hospital
examples.challenge: Prior authorization ownership fragmented across many clinical and scheduling staff. Process lacked visibility, documentation discipline, and operational control.
examples.scale: 88 staff members involved in prior auth before redesign.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: Staff working on prior auth fell from 88 to 5. 30,000 clinical hours saved per year. Denials dropped materially.
examples.whyItMatters: Exactly the kind of workflow where clinicians are trapped in administrative coordination that should never have required them.

examples.name: East Coast Orthopedic Group Prior Authorization
examples.source: Infinx case study on an East Coast orthopedic group
examples.challenge: Losing time on prior authorization review, eligibility checks, and payer-guideline lookups.
examples.scale: More than 600 prior authorizations processed per month.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: 80+ staff hours saved per week (~$70K+ annually). Additional $55,000 in revenue in first 3 months from expanded hours and fewer lost patients.
examples.whyItMatters: Value is not just efficiency — it is faster patient access and lower revenue leakage from avoidable delays.

examples.name: National Prior Authorization Burden in Medicare Advantage
examples.source: KFF analysis of 2024 Medicare Advantage prior authorization; AMA 2024 survey
examples.challenge: Prior authorization remains a huge administrative tax on providers and staff.
examples.scale: Nearly 53 million prior auth requests submitted to Medicare Advantage in 2024. 4.1 million denied. Physicians: average 43 prior auths per week.
examples.humanHoursAndDollarImpact: 12 physician-and-staff hours per week per physician. More than a third of physicians employ staff who work exclusively on prior authorization.
examples.whyItMatters: Not a niche back-office annoyance. One of the most concentrated pools of repetitive administrative friction in healthcare.

quickStart: Prior auth determination and packet prep
avoid: Clinical decision support framed as care guidance
Workflows requiring deeply custom payer logic on day one
consultantSignals: McKinsey: Health systems spend more than $140 billion annually on revenue cycle. Nearly 20% of claims denied on average, as many as 60% never appealed.
McKinsey recommendation: Start with the highest-volume, most error-prone processes with clear, quantifiable outcomes.
AHA 2025: Prior authorization and denial prevention are real but medium-readiness AI targets that depend on workflow maturity and data availability.
obstacles: Fragmented payer portals and payer-specific rules
Provider skepticism if system creates more clicks
Long sales cycles and high proof requirements
whereDataLives: Fax inboxes and scanned clinical documents
openQuestions: Which service lines have the worst prior-auth pain today?
What share of denials are avoidable at the front end?
Which payers and portals dominate the workload?
Who owns the pain and budget: rev cycle, patient access, or department ops?
What audit trail is required for compliance and appeals?
How much human review is mandatory before a packet is submitted?
